b: ^Mrs Kowalski: [Glances over from her pillow] You're the one from IT?
^Mrs Kowalski: Have you got a moment?

first_words: ^Mrs Kowalski: Feels like you've all been up all night dealing with this mess.
^Mrs Kowalski: I just wanted to say — they're doing their best, the nurses. Don't blame them if things slip.

c-0: ^Mrs Kowalski: The ward was chaos when I woke up. No monitors, staff running around with clipboards.
^Mrs Kowalski: But Sarah — the charge nurse — she didn't panic. That matters.
c-1: ^Mrs Kowalski: Post-op, so not great. But stable. My daughter would be asking all the questions you probably should be asking.
^Mrs Kowalski: She's not here yet, so I'm stuck doing it.

pump_concern: ^Mrs Kowalski: That pump — the one beside the bed. I've been watching it all morning.
^Mrs Kowalski: It's beeping differently now. And there's a new rate on the screen.
^Mrs Kowalski: [Points with her hand] Could you get someone to check it?

c-0: ^Mrs Kowalski: I don't know. I'm just a patient. But I know my body.
^Mrs Kowalski: The last bag was running slow and steady. This one feels different.
^Mrs Kowalski: Maybe it's nothing. But maybe it's worth having someone look.
c-1: ^Mrs Kowalski: Thank you. I don't like making a fuss, but Sarah's been pulled in three directions.
^Mrs Kowalski: Someone needs to pay attention to what's being put into my arm.
c-2: ^Mrs Kowalski: [Settles back against her pillow, quietly] Okay. I hope you're right.

monitoring_discussed: ^Mrs Kowalski: This morning I was on continuous monitors. Everything tracked on that big screen.
^Mrs Kowalski: Then the ransomware hit and suddenly it's back to how hospitals used to be — someone checking me every fifteen minutes.
^Mrs Kowalski: Which means if something happens between checks...

c-0: ^Mrs Kowalski: I know they are. But there's a reason they invented continuous monitoring.
^Mrs Kowalski: I'm post-op cardiac. I'm supposed to have an alarm on my chest and a screen showing my heart rate.
^Mrs Kowalski: Instead I have a clipboard and hope.
c-1: ^Mrs Kowalski: My oxygen saturation. If that drops and nobody's watching, I won't know. Neither will they — until it's late.
^Mrs Kowalski: Sarah's nurse checked me a few minutes ago. I was doing fine then.
^Mrs Kowalski: But things change fast after surgery.
c-2: ^Mrs Kowalski: I hope so. [Pulls the blanket up] I just don't want to be the reason you all have a bad day at work.

b: ^Mrs Kowalski: The pharmacist came by earlier with the charge nurse. They looked worried.
^Mrs Kowalski: Is something wrong with my medication?
^Mrs Kowalski: [Quietly anxious] The pharmacist just told me they have to do manual dosing instead of the pump.
^Mrs Kowalski: I'm trying not to worry. But "manual dosing" after someone hacked the system — that's a lot to process.
^The manual approach is actually safer right now

c-0: ^Mrs Kowalski: How is manual safer than a machine programmed to do it?
^Mrs Kowalski: [Pauses] I suppose a human can see what they're doing. A machine just does what it's told.
c-1: ^Mrs Kowalski: I know. The pharmacist explained. She's going to watch every dose.
^Mrs Kowalski: It's strange to have someone that focused on what goes into my arm. Not bad strange, just... different.

b:
^Mrs Kowalski: I think so? Someone verified something on a computer and the charge nurse looked relieved.

^Mrs Kowalski: They're still being careful but I'm not as worried now.
^Mrs Kowalski: I don't think so yet. Which is why I'm being very careful about what I ask for.

patient_advocacy: ^Mrs Kowalski: You know what Sarah said to me this morning? "Your safety is the only thing that matters to me right now."
^Mrs Kowalski: And she meant it. Even with the monitors down, the ransomware, the staffing stretched thin.
^Mrs Kowalski: I'm just one patient in one bed, and she's still putting that first.

c-0: ^Mrs Kowalski: Yes. I worked in hospitals once, years ago. Not as a nurse — admin — but I remember that feeling.
^Mrs Kowalski: That's why I'm speaking up about the pump. Not to cause trouble, but because she needs to know.
c-1: ^Mrs Kowalski: She is. And so are you, from what I can see.
^Mrs Kowalski: It's not just the IT people who matter. It's people willing to say when something doesn't feel right.

hub: ^How are you coping without continuous monitoring?
^Thank you for looking out for the ward

c-0: ^Mrs Kowalski: Just watching my infusion pump. It's new today — different bag.
^Mrs Kowalski: I'm sure it's fine. But if you could look at it...
c-1: ^Mrs Kowalski: Did someone check it? I don't mean to nag, but I've been watching it all morning.
c-4: ^Mrs Kowalski: [Smiles weakly] That's what we do for each other, isn't it?
^Mrs Kowalski: Even when you're just a patient in a bed.
c-5: ^Mrs Kowalski: [Settles back against her pillow] Be safe.

state_stable: ^Mrs Kowalski: The lady in the next bed was asking for the nurse earlier. She seemed a bit groggy after her operation, but they said that's normal.
state_sedated: ^Mrs Kowalski: I'm not sure she's alright. She was trying to call out but she can't seem to wake up properly. Is that normal?
state_critical: ^Mrs Kowalski: [Voice urgent] Please, someone needs to look at her — she's not responding at all! I've been pressing the call bell but nobody's coming!
state_deceased: ^Mrs Kowalski: [Hands shaking, voice barely audible] She stopped breathing. I kept pressing the bell. I kept pressing it. Nobody came in time.
